questions.question: What type of version control system is Git?
questions.answer: Distributed version control system

questions.question: What is a Git repository?
options: A remote server that stores backups of your code
A project tracked by Git containing your files plus a hidden .git folder with the entire history
A folder that only contains source code files
A text file that lists all your project dependencies
questions.answer: A project tracked by Git containing your files plus a hidden .git folder with the entire history

questions.question: What is a commit in Git?
options: A request to merge two branches together
A snapshot of your project at a point in time, with a message describing what changed
A copy of a file saved to the cloud
A command that uploads your code to a remote server
questions.answer: A snapshot of your project at a point in time, with a message describing what changed

questions.question: What is the staging area (index) in Git?
options: The remote server where your code is stored
The folder where deleted files are kept
A holding area where you prepare changes before committing them
The main branch of your repository
questions.answer: A holding area where you prepare changes before committing them

questions.question: What is the correct order of the basic Git workflow?
options: Commit snapshot → Edit files → Stage changes
Stage changes → Edit files → Commit snapshot
Edit files → Commit snapshot → Stage changes
Edit files → Stage changes → Commit snapshot
questions.answer: Edit files → Stage changes → Commit snapshot

questions.question: Which command checks whether Git is installed on your machine?
questions.answer: git --version

questions.question: What does the 'git help' command do?
options: Opens a web browser to the Git documentation site
Prints a summary of the most common Git commands
Creates a help file in your repository
Sends an error report to the Git developers
questions.answer: Prints a summary of the most common Git commands

questions.question: Which command opens the manual page for the 'git init' command?
questions.answer: git help init

questions.question: Why must you configure your name and email before making commits?
options: Git uses them to encrypt your files
Git needs to know who you are so it can attribute commits
They are required to install Git
They are used as your login credentials for GitHub
questions.answer: Git needs to know who you are so it can attribute commits

questions.question: Which command sets your Git username globally?
questions.answer: git config --global user.name "Your Name"

questions.question: What does the '--global' flag mean in 'git config --global'?
options: The setting applies only to the current repository
The setting applies to all repositories on your machine
The setting is shared with all Git users on the internet
The setting applies only to remote repositories
questions.answer: The setting applies to all repositories on your machine

questions.question: Which command displays all Git configuration values on your machine?
questions.answer: git config --list

questions.question: Which command sets the default text editor Git will use?
questions.answer: git config --global core.editor "nano"

questions.question: When does Git open a text editor?
options: For tasks like writing commit messages and resolving merge conflicts
questions.answer: For tasks like writing commit messages and resolving merge conflicts

questions.question: What is the modern convention for the default branch name?
questions.answer: main

questions.question: Which command sets the default branch name for new repositories to 'main'?
questions.answer: git config --global init.defaultBranch main

questions.question: Which command initializes a new Git repository in the current directory?
questions.answer: git init

questions.question: What hidden folder does Git create when you initialize a repository?
questions.answer: .git

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT typically found inside the .git directory?
questions.answer: README.md

questions.question: Why should you avoid manually editing files inside the .git directory?
options: The files are encrypted and cannot be read
It could corrupt your repository's history and data
The files are automatically regenerated every time you run a command
The .git directory is read-only by default
questions.answer: It could corrupt your repository's history and data

questions.question: What does 'git status' tell you?
options: The total size of your repository on disk
What branch you're on, what's staged, modified, and untracked
How many collaborators have access to the repository
The date the repository was created
questions.answer: What branch you're on, what's staged, modified, and untracked

questions.question: Before running 'git add', a newly created file shows up in 'git status' as what?
questions.answer: Untracked

questions.question: What does 'git add README.md' do?
options: Creates a new file called README.md
Moves README.md to the staging area so it is ready to be committed
Commits README.md directly to the repository
Deletes README.md from the working directory
questions.answer: Moves README.md to the staging area so it is ready to be committed

questions.question: What does the '-m' flag do in 'git commit -m "Initial commit"'?
options: Merges the commit into the main branch
Marks the commit as a milestone
Lets you provide the commit message inline without opening an editor
Makes the commit a minor (non-breaking) change
questions.answer: Lets you provide the commit message inline without opening an editor

questions.question: What does 'git log' display?
options: A list of all files currently in the repository
The commit history including hash, author, date, and message
A log of all commands you have run in the terminal
A list of all branches in the repository
questions.answer: The commit history including hash, author, date, and message
